docs.title: Migration guide: swarmline v1.4 → v1.5
docs.text:
This guide covers every user-visible change between swarmline 1.4.x and 1.5.0. Most of v1.5.0 is additive — new runtimes, new tools, multimodal input, parallel agents. The three changes that warrant attention are:

  1. The default runtime flipped from claude_sdk to thin.
  2. Python 3.10 is no longer supported. Minimum is now >=3.11.
  3. serve.create_app(allow_unauthenticated_query=True) requires explicit host= (security audit closure, P2 #5).

If your code pins runtime= explicitly, your interpreter is already >=3.11, and you do not use the unauthenticated /v1/query endpoint, the upgrade is drop-in.

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text:
• Pin runtime= explicitly in AgentConfig to avoid surprise from the new default.
  • Upgrade your interpreter to Python 3.11 or newer.
  • If you use serve.create_app(allow_unauthenticated_query=True), add host="127.0.0.1" (or another loopback) — the legacy signature without host= is now a hard error.
  • AgentConfig.max_thinking_tokens is deprecated — start migrating to thinking= (typed).
  • All other changes are purely additive: opt in when you need them.

docs.title: 1. Default runtime: claude_sdk → thin
docs.text:
Before (v1.4.x):

from swarmline.agent import Agent, AgentConfig

agent = Agent(AgentConfig(system_prompt="You are a helpful assistant."))
# Implicitly runtime="claude_sdk" — required `claude-agent-sdk` to be installed.

After (v1.5.0):

from swarmline.agent import Agent, AgentConfig

agent = Agent(AgentConfig(system_prompt="You are a helpful assistant."))
# Now runtime="thin" — uses ThinRuntime with multi-provider support.

Why this changed: the in-tree thin runtime now reaches feature parity with the Claude Agent SDK adapter on hooks, tool policy, subagents, slash-commands, native tool calling, multimodal, and session resume. It is a better default because it works with Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, DeepSeek, and OpenAI-compatible providers out of the box, without requiring the claude_agent_sdk package on PyPI.

How to migrate:

If you depend on claude_sdk-specific behaviour, pin it explicitly:

agent = Agent(AgentConfig(
    system_prompt="...",
    runtime="claude_sdk",   # restores v1.4 behaviour
))

Otherwise, the new thin default works for most users; we recommend pinning it anyway to make the choice durable across future major releases:

agent = Agent(AgentConfig(
    system_prompt="...",
    runtime="thin",         # explicit lock to current default
))

docs.title: 2. Python 3.10 is no longer supported
docs.text: pyproject.toml now declares requires-python = ">=3.11" and the publish workflow no longer tests against 3.10. Upgrade your interpreter before installing swarmline 1.5.0.

docs.title: 3. serve.create_app requires explicit host= for unauthenticated mode
docs.text:
Audit closure P2 #5 (HIGH severity). When allow_unauthenticated_query=True and auth_token=None, the v1.4.x signature create_app(agent, allow_unauthenticated_query=True) (without host=) emitted a deprecation warning but still constructed the app. This permitted operators to combine the legacy call with uvicorn --host 0.0.0.0 and silently expose unauthenticated /v1/query publicly. v1.5.0 graduates the warning to a hard ValueError.

Before (v1.4.x — deprecated, still worked):

from swarmline.serve.app import create_app

app = create_app(agent, allow_unauthenticated_query=True)
# DeprecationWarning emitted; app still constructed.

After (v1.5.0 — raises ValueError):

from swarmline.serve.app import create_app

# Local-only mode: explicit loopback host
app = create_app(
    agent,
    allow_unauthenticated_query=True,
    host="127.0.0.1",   # required since v1.5.0
)

# Production mode: token auth (host enforcement bypassed by auth)
app = create_app(agent, auth_token="<server-token>")

Allowed loopback hosts: 127.0.0.1, localhost, ::1. Any other host raises ValueError at factory time. If you intentionally need an unauthenticated public listener, configure auth_token and an external proxy with its own auth layer instead — there is no supported "unauthenticated + public" combination by design.

docs.title: 4. LLM error message language
docs.text:
ThinLlmError messages emitted from runtime/thin/errors.py are now in English. This is only noticeable if you grep on the literal string in logs or tests:

Before After Ошибка LLM API (anthropic): … LLM API error (anthropic): …

If you have log filters or tests that match the Russian string, update them.

docs.title: Deprecations (still functional, will be removed in a future release)
AgentConfig.max_thinking_tokens → thinking=
docs.text:
The dedicated integer field is being phased out in favour of a structured thinking parameter that accepts either a dict or one of the typed dataclasses exported from swarmline.runtime.types.

Old (still works in 1.5.0):

AgentConfig(system_prompt="...", max_thinking_tokens=4096)

New (preferred):

# Dict form — accepted but not type-checked.
AgentConfig(
    system_prompt="...",
    thinking={"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": 4096},
)

# Typed form — type-checked, IDE-friendly.
from swarmline.runtime.types import ThinkingConfigEnabled

AgentConfig(
    system_prompt="...",
    thinking=ThinkingConfigEnabled(type="enabled", budget_tokens=4096),
)

Other variants:

  • ThinkingConfigAdaptive(type="adaptive") — let the model decide.
  • ThinkingConfigDisabled(type="disabled") — explicitly disable thinking.

The thinking= argument also takes precedence when both are passed.

docs.title: New features (additive — no migration required)
docs.text: You can adopt any of these incrementally. None of them changes existing behaviour unless you opt in.

docs.title: Foundation filters (Phase 11)
docs.text:
from swarmline import ProjectInstructionFilter
from swarmline.runtime.types import RuntimeConfig

config = RuntimeConfig(
    model="sonnet",
    input_filters=(ProjectInstructionFilter(),),
)

ProjectInstructionFilter walks up from the current working directory, loading CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, RULES.md, and GEMINI.md into the system prompt — the same convention that Claude Code uses.

docs.title: Tool surface expansion (Phase 12)
docs.text:
• web_fetch now respects web_allowed_domains / web_blocked_domains.
  • read_mcp_resource builtin tool reads MCP resources by URI.
  • list_resources / read_resource MCP helpers with TTL caching.

docs.title: Conversation compaction (Phase 13)
docs.text:
When the message history grows past a threshold, ThinRuntime can collapse it through three levels: tool-result truncation → LLM summarisation → emergency truncation. Configure via CompactionConfig:

from swarmline.runtime.thin.compaction import CompactionConfig

config = RuntimeConfig(
    model="sonnet",
    compaction=CompactionConfig(
        threshold_tokens=80_000,
        target_tokens=20_000,
    ),
)

docs.title: Session resume (Phase 14)
docs.text:
from swarmline.agent import Agent, AgentConfig

agent = Agent(AgentConfig(system_prompt="..."))
session_id = "user-42-thread-1"

# First conversation — auto-persisted to JSONL.
async with agent.conversation(session_id=session_id) as conv:
    await conv.send("Hi, I'm Anton.")

# Later, in a new process, resume the same session.
async with agent.conversation(session_id=session_id) as conv:
    reply = await conv.send("Do you remember my name?")

JsonlMessageStore writes append-only JSONL files (SHA-256 filenames, corrupted-line resilience). Auto-compaction kicks in when the session grows past the threshold.

docs.title: Thinking events (Phase 15)
docs.text: Models that emit extended-thinking blocks now surface them as RuntimeEvent.thinking_delta events in addition to the regular text deltas. The LlmCallResult envelope captures thinking content end-to-end. AnthropicAdapter extracts the dedicated thinking blocks; other providers fall through transparently.

docs.title: Multimodal input (Phase 16)
docs.text:
import base64
from swarmline.domain_types import Message, TextBlock, ImageBlock

with open("diagram.png", "rb") as f:
    image_b64 = base64.b64encode(f.read()).decode()

message = Message(
    role="user",
    content="What's in this image?",
    content_blocks=[
        TextBlock(text="What's in this image?"),
        ImageBlock(data=image_b64, media_type="image/png"),
    ],
)

Message.content_blocks is an additive field — the existing text field still works and is treated as a single TextBlock internally. Provider converters target Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google formats. PDF and Jupyter notebook extractors are bundled.

docs.title: Parallel agents (Phase 17)
docs.text:
from swarmline.runtime.thin.subagent_tool import SubagentToolConfig

agent = Agent(AgentConfig(
    system_prompt="...",
    subagent_config=SubagentToolConfig(
        isolation="worktree",   # each spawned subagent gets its own git worktree
    ),
))

When isolation="worktree", every spawn_agent invocation runs in a fresh git worktree on a temporary branch and is cleaned up automatically when no changes were made. Spawned agents emit RuntimeEvent.background_complete when they finish; the new monitor_agent builtin tool lets the parent agent track their progress.

docs.title: New runtimes
docs.text:
• runtime="openai_agents" — OpenAI Agents SDK (Codex + OpenAI models). See the agent SDK for deep tool integration and Codex-specific features.
  • runtime="pi_sdk" — Pi SDK via a Node.js bridge. Useful when you want Pi's planning model in the loop.

Both runtimes are registered through the standard RuntimeRegistry. Install their optional extras (swarmline[openai-agents], swarmline[pi-sdk]) to pull the dependencies in.

docs.title: Agent packs
docs.text:
Declarative agent bundles let you ship a packaged agent definition (system prompt, tools, runtime config) as a single resource:

from swarmline.agent_pack import AgentPackResolver

resolver = AgentPackResolver()
pack = resolver.resolve("github://acme/agents/qa-bot@v1")
agent = pack.build_agent()

See docs/agent-pack.md for the resource layout.

docs.title: JSONL telemetry sink
docs.text:
from swarmline.observability.event_bus import InMemoryEventBus
from swarmline.observability.jsonl_sink import JsonlTelemetrySink

bus = InMemoryEventBus()
sink = JsonlTelemetrySink(path="logs/events.jsonl", event_bus=bus)
await sink.start()  # auto-subscribes to bus, writes append-only JSONL

I/O is wrapped in asyncio.to_thread(); concurrent writes are serialised with an asyncio.Lock. Built-in key-name redaction strips secrets from records before they hit disk.

docs.title: Coding profile
docs.text:
from swarmline.runtime.thin.coding_profile import CodingProfileConfig

agent = Agent(AgentConfig(
    system_prompt="You write Python.",
    coding_profile=CodingProfileConfig(),  # enables sandbox + web + thinking with hardened policy
))

docs.title: Bug fixes that may affect users
Test isolation (C-1 / C-3)
docs.text:
If you ran swarmline-using tests in your CI and saw weird "logs end up on stdout" or "tests pollute each other's loggers" issues — those were tracked as C-1 and C-3. Fixed in 1.5.0 by:

  • removing logging.basicConfig(force=True) from observability/logger.py (now idempotent and only configures when no handlers exist),
  • routing stdlib logging to stderr (was stdout), so swarmline … --json writes clean JSON to stdout,
  • routing structlog to stderr as well via PrintLoggerFactory(file=sys.stderr),
  • adding a _reset_root_logger autouse fixture in tests/conftest.py for clean test cross-pollination.

If your application configured logging through swarmline's helper and relied on the old force=True behaviour, you may need to call logging.basicConfig(...) in your own bootstrap.

docs.title: Non-blocking telemetry (C-2)
docs.text: JsonlTelemetrySink.record() no longer blocks the event loop on writes. If you have measured event-loop stalls during heavy logging in 1.4, those should be gone. No code change required.

docs.title: Verifying the upgrade
docs.text:
pip install --upgrade swarmline
python -c "import swarmline; print(swarmline.__version__)"  # 1.5.0

# Run your test suite — most users see no diff.
pytest -q

# If something broke around `runtime=`, the most common cause is the default flip.
# Pin it explicitly and re-run.

docs.title: Need help?
docs.text:
• Full release notes: CHANGELOG.md [1.5.0] section.
  • Issues: https://github.com/fockus/swarmline/issues.
  • Older migration notes: docs/migration-guide.md (covers v1.3 → v1.4 secure-by-default and v0.5 → v1.0 legacy migration).

docs.title: Proposal: Scratchpad Module
docs.text: Status: Draft Author: Anton Ivanov Date: 2026-04-10 Related: Knowledge Bank, Memory, MCP Server

docs.title: Motivation
docs.text:
Agents need ephemeral within-run working memory for intermediate state: research notes, observations, hypotheses, partial results. This is different from:

  • Memory (cross-run, persistent) — what the agent remembers long-term
  • Knowledge Bank (structured, domain docs) — curated knowledge with metadata
  • Todo (task checklist) — what needs to be done

Scratchpad is the agent's notepad during a single task execution — analogous to how Manus maintains a working file system, or how a human developer keeps scratch notes while debugging.

docs.title: Inspiration
docs.text: System Working Memory Manus File system: agent writes/reads files, sees descriptions in context Claude Code TodoWrite/TodoRead + file system OpenViking AGFS write(), read(), ls(), grep() on namespaced file system Swarmline (current) No scratchpad — only persistent memory and todo

docs.title: API (4 tools)
docs.text:
# Write/update a scratchpad entry
scratch_write(key: str, content: str, tags: list[str] = []) -> str

# Read one entry or list all
scratch_read(key: str | None = None) -> str

# Search entries by content/key/tags
scratch_search(query: str) -> str

# Delete an entry
scratch_delete(key: str) -> str

docs.title: Data Model
docs.text:
@dataclass
class ScratchpadEntry:
    key: str                    # unique identifier (e.g., "research_notes", "draft_v2")
    content: str                # the actual content
    tags: tuple[str, ...] = ()  # optional tags for filtering
    created_at: datetime
    updated_at: datetime

docs.title: Providers (pluggable backend)
docs.text: Provider Use Case Persistence InMemoryScratchpadProvider Single-run, testing None (lost on exit) OpenVikingScratchpadProvider Production with OV AGFS namespace + semantic search FilesystemScratchpadProvider Local dev, CLI agents JSON files on disk DatabaseScratchpadProvider Production without OV SQLAlchemy async

docs.title: OpenViking Integration
docs.text:
When OpenViking is available, the scratchpad maps directly to AGFS:

viking://tenant/{tid}/workspace/{wid}/scratchpad/
  ├── research_notes.md      ← scratch_write("research_notes", "...")
  ├── draft_v2.md            ← scratch_write("draft_v2", "...")
  └── .meta/                 ← tags, timestamps (JSON sidecar)

Key benefit: entries are auto-indexed by OpenViking for semantic search. workspace_search("API design patterns") finds relevant scratchpad notes alongside files and artifacts.

docs.title: Context Injection
docs.text:
Scratchpad summary injected into system prompt (similar to todo recitation):

[Scratchpad: 3 entries]
- research_notes (updated 2m ago, tags: market, analysis)
- competitor_list (updated 5m ago, tags: research)
- draft_outline (updated 1m ago, tags: report)

Agent sees entry keys + metadata. To read content, uses scratch_read("research_notes").

docs.title: Module Structure
docs.text:
packages/swarmline/src/swarmline/scratchpad/
  ├── __init__.py           # public API: create_scratchpad_tools, providers
  ├── types.py              # ScratchpadEntry, ScratchpadConfig
  ├── provider.py           # InMemoryScratchpadProvider
  ├── openviking_provider.py # OpenVikingScratchpadProvider (AGFS-backed)
  ├── fs_provider.py        # FilesystemScratchpadProvider
  ├── db_provider.py        # DatabaseScratchpadProvider (SQLAlchemy)
  └── tools.py              # create_scratchpad_tools() → ToolSpec + executors

docs.title: MCP Server Integration
docs.text:
Scratchpad tools exposed through Swarmline MCP server (headless mode):

# In swarmline/mcp/_server.py, register scratchpad tools alongside memory/plan/team
@mcp.tool()
async def swarmline_scratch_write(key: str, content: str, tags: list[str] = []) -> dict: ...

@mcp.tool()
async def swarmline_scratch_read(key: str | None = None) -> dict: ...

docs.title: Implementation Plan
docs.text:
1. Core types + InMemory provider — types.py, provider.py (~1h)
  2. Tools — tools.py with create_scratchpad_tools() (~1h)
  3. OpenViking provider — openviking_provider.py using AGFS (~2h)
  4. DB provider — db_provider.py for non-OV production (~1h)
  5. MCP integration — register in _server.py headless mode (~30m)
  6. Context injection — scratchpad summary in system prompt (~30m)
  7. Tests — unit + integration for each provider (~2h)

Total: ~8 hours

docs.title: Relationship to OpenViking
docs.text:
Scratchpad is a Swarmline-level abstraction that can use OpenViking as a backend. This keeps Swarmline independent (no hard OV dependency) while leveraging OV when available:

Agent → Swarmline Scratchpad API → InMemoryProvider (default)
                                → OpenVikingScratchpadProvider (when OV available)
                                → DatabaseProvider (when DB available)

The OpenViking provider maps scratchpad operations to AGFS calls, getting semantic search for free via OV's indexing pipeline.

docs.title: openai_agents runtime
docs.text:
The openai_agents runtime is a thin wrapper around the official OpenAI Agents SDK — it implements Swarmline's AgentRuntime contract by delegating to Runner.run_streamed() and converting Agents-SDK stream events to RuntimeEvent.

Use it when you want:

  • The OpenAI Agents SDK's tool-handling and streaming behaviour.
  • First-class Codex MCP integration (planning + code edits in one loop).
  • Access to OpenAI's models with their native function-calling.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
pip install "swarmline[openai-agents]"

This pulls in openai-agents (the SDK).

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
from swarmline.agent import Agent, AgentConfig

agent = Agent(AgentConfig(
    system_prompt="You are a focused engineering assistant.",
    runtime="openai_agents",
    model="gpt-4.1",
))

reply = await agent.query("Write a Python script that prints prime numbers up to 100.")
print(reply.text)

Streaming, tool calls, hooks, and structured output all work the same way as with runtime="thin" — the wrapper is in charge of mapping events.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
OpenAIAgentsConfig lives in swarmline.runtime.openai_agents.types and lets you customise the underlying Agent and optionally attach Codex as an MCP server:

from swarmline.agent import Agent, AgentConfig
from swarmline.runtime.openai_agents.types import OpenAIAgentsConfig

agents_config = OpenAIAgentsConfig(
    model="gpt-4.1",                       # default model the SDK uses
    codex_enabled=True,                    # attach Codex MCP server (planning + code edits)
    codex_sandbox="network-off",           # Codex sandbox profile
    codex_approval_policy="suggest",       # how Codex requests approval for risky calls
    max_turns=20,                          # OpenAI Agents SDK turn cap
    env={"OPENAI_API_KEY": "sk-..."},      # injected into the SDK runtime
)

agent = Agent(AgentConfig(
    system_prompt="...",
    runtime="openai_agents",
    runtime_options=agents_config,   # passed through to OpenAIAgentsRuntime
    model="gpt-4.1",
))

When codex_enabled=True, the runtime registers Codex as an MCP server inside the OpenAI Agent, so the LLM can call its planning and code-execution tools alongside any tools you define in AgentConfig.tools. The codex_sandbox and codex_approval_policy fields are passed through to Codex unchanged.

docs.title: Capabilities
docs.text:
The runtime registers under RuntimeRegistry["openai_agents"] with these capability flags (queryable via swarmline.runtime.factory.runtime_capabilities()):

Capability Value runtime_name openai_agents tier premium streaming yes tool_calling yes parallel_tool_calling yes hooks yes (mapped via Swarmline HookRegistry) structured_output yes (Pydantic models supported) multimodal yes (via ContentBlock)

docs.title: Provider keys
docs.text: The Agents SDK reads OPENAI_API_KEY from the environment (or whatever the SDK documents). Swarmline does not hold its own per-runtime key — set the env var once and any OpenAI-backed runtime picks it up.

docs.title: Tool bridging
docs.text:
User-defined @tool-decorated callables are converted to OpenAI Agents SDK tools via swarmline.runtime.openai_agents.tool_bridge.toolspecs_to_agent_tools(). Each tool's JSON Schema (auto-inferred from type hints + docstring) becomes the SDK's tool schema verbatim, and the executor wires the agent-side handler back to the original Python function.

from swarmline.agent.tool import tool

@tool
def lookup_user(user_id: str) -> dict:
    """Fetch a user record by ID."""
    return db.get(user_id)

agent = Agent(AgentConfig(
    system_prompt="...",
    runtime="openai_agents",
    tools=(lookup_user,),
))

The LLM sees a normal OpenAI tool definition; the executor translates the SDK's tool-call event back into a Python call to lookup_user.

docs.title: Streaming
docs.text:
OpenAIAgentsRuntime.run() yields RuntimeEvents for:

  • assistant_delta — partial text from Runner.run_streamed().
  • tool_call / tool_result — individual tool invocations.
  • thinking_delta — when the model emits explicit reasoning blocks.
  • final — accumulated text + TurnMetrics (token counts, cost if known).
  • error — RuntimeErrorData mapped from any SDK exception.

docs.title: Differences from runtime="thin"
docs.text:
Concern thin openai_agents Provider matrix Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, DeepSeek OpenAI only Tool dispatch Swarmline ToolExecutor OpenAI Agents SDK RunSession Hook support full (Pre/Post/Stop/UserPromptSubmit) mapped to SDK events Native parallel tools yes (Phase 5) yes (SDK feature) Subagent (spawn_agent) yes yes (works via tool registration) Codex MCP available via mcp_servers={...} first-class via enable_codex=True Cost tracking pricing.json inherited from pricing.json

If you don't need Codex or the OpenAI Agents SDK specifically, prefer the default runtime="thin" for portability across providers.

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• The runtime depends on the OpenAI Agents SDK; if it's not installed, initialising the runtime emits a dependency_missing RuntimeEvent.error instead of running.
  • Cancellation goes through the SDK's run object — long-running tools that ignore SDK cancellation will not stop until they return.

docs.title: pi_sdk runtime
docs.text:
The pi_sdk runtime is a thin Python wrapper that launches a Node.js subprocess ("the bridge") which embeds Mario Zechner's @mariozechner/pi-coding-agent SDK. The Python side owns the Swarmline contracts; the Node side owns the actual model loop.

Use it when you want:

  • Pi's coding-agent loop and tool semantics, but inside a Swarmline pipeline.
  • A clean way to mix Pi's planning model with the rest of the Swarmline ecosystem (memory, observability, hooks, JSONL telemetry).

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
You need:

  • Node.js ≥ 20.6 on PATH.
  • The Node package @mariozechner/pi-coding-agent available to the bridge. The bridge resolves it relative to its own directory, so installing the package globally or in the bridge's node_modules is enough.
  • The Python optional extras:
pip install "swarmline[pi-sdk]"

The Node bridge ships inside the swarmline distribution under runtime/pi_sdk/bridge/. The runtime spawns it via node.

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
from swarmline.agent import Agent, AgentConfig

agent = Agent(AgentConfig(
    system_prompt="You are a focused coding assistant.",
    runtime="pi_sdk",
    model="claude-sonnet-4",   # any model Pi supports
))

reply = await agent.query("Refactor calc.py to extract validation into a helper.")
print(reply.text)

If node is not installed or not on PATH, the runtime emits a dependency_missing error event with a clear message — it does not crash.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
PiSdkOptions lives in swarmline.runtime.pi_sdk.types:

from swarmline.agent import Agent, AgentConfig
from swarmline.runtime.pi_sdk.types import PiSdkOptions

pi_options = PiSdkOptions(
    toolset="coding",                       # "none" | "readonly" | "coding"
    coding_profile=None,                    # name of a bundled Pi coding profile, optional
    cwd="/abs/path/to/project",             # workdir for the bridge subprocess
    agent_dir=None,                         # override Pi agent dir
    auth_file=None,                         # path to Pi auth json, optional
    session_mode="memory",                  # "memory" | "persisted"
    bridge_command=(),                      # override the node command, default uses bundled bridge
    package_name="@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent",
    provider=None,                          # let Pi pick from its own config
    model_id=None,                          # let Pi pick
    thinking_level=None,                    # e.g. "low" | "medium" | "high" if supported
    timeout_seconds=300.0,                  # per-turn wall-clock cap
)

agent = Agent(AgentConfig(
    system_prompt="...",
    runtime="pi_sdk",
    runtime_options=pi_options,
    model="claude-sonnet-4",
))

If you don't pass runtime_options, the runtime derives sensible defaults from the RuntimeConfig (model name, env vars, base_url) — most users shouldn't need to touch PiSdkOptions at all.

docs.title: How the bridge works
docs.text:
1. PiSdkRuntime.run() builds a JSON request describing the turn (messages, system_prompt, tools, config).
  2. It launches node <bridge.js> as a subprocess via asyncio.create_subprocess_exec().
  3. The bridge reads the request from stdin, calls Pi's SDK, and emits one newline-delimited JSON event per line on stdout.
  4. The Python side parses each line through event_mapper.map_pi_bridge_event() and yields a RuntimeEvent.

Every event the bridge emits — assistant delta, tool call, tool result, error, final — has a 1:1 mapping to a Swarmline RuntimeEvent, so pipelines, hooks, and observability work exactly as with runtime="thin".

docs.title: Capabilities
docs.text: Capability Value runtime_name pi_sdk tier premium streaming yes tool_calling yes parallel_tool_calling inherited from Pi hooks yes (mapped on the Python side) structured_output yes (Pydantic via post-validate) multimodal yes (Pi handles content_blocks)

docs.title: Tool bridging
docs.text:
@tool-decorated callables are serialized to the bridge as JSON Schema. The Node bridge registers them with Pi's tool runner; when Pi calls one, the bridge emits a tool_call event, the Python runtime executes the callable locally, and the bridge receives the tool_result event back over a follow-up message.

from swarmline.agent.tool import tool

@tool
def query_db(table: str, where: str) -> list[dict]:
    """Run a select against the database."""
    return repository.query(table, where)

agent = Agent(AgentConfig(
    system_prompt="...",
    runtime="pi_sdk",
    tools=(query_db,),
))

This means tool execution is always local to the Python process. The Node bridge is dumb on tools — it just relays calls and results. Sandbox policies, async semantics, and security checks all run in Python, so nothing changes relative to the thin runtime.

docs.title: Cancellation
docs.text: AgentRuntime.cancel() sets a flag that the next event loop iteration honours, and additionally sends SIGTERM to the Node subprocess. If the bridge is in the middle of a tool call that ignores cancellation, you may see a few more events arrive before the process actually exits. The runtime always cleans up the subprocess in a finally block.

docs.title: Differences from runtime="thin"
docs.text:
Concern thin pi_sdk LLM client direct Python call Node subprocess via Pi Provider matrix Anthropic / OpenAI / Google / DeepSeek whatever Pi supports Latency overhead none ~50-100ms subprocess startup per turn Process model in-process one Node subprocess per turn Hooks first-class mapped from bridge events Cost tracking pricing.json inherited

For most projects, runtime="thin" is faster and simpler. Reach for pi_sdk when you specifically want Pi's loop semantics or you're already running Pi as a coding agent and want to integrate it into Swarmline.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Symptom Cause / fix dependency_missing: Node.js is required for runtime='pi_sdk' Install Node.js ≥ 20.6 and ensure node is on PATH. runtime_crash: Failed to launch PI SDK bridge: ... Inspect the message; usually node_modules lookup failure for @mariozechner/pi-coding-agent. Bridge appears hung / no events Check the subprocess stderr (it surfaces in the runtime log). Increase timeout_seconds if the model is genuinely slow. Tool call returns immediately with {"status":"error"} The tool's executor raised. The Python-side error message is in the result.
